quote: GPT-5 will still, like its predecessors, be a bull in a china shop, reckless and hard to control
claim: GPT-5 will remain difficult to control and prone to reckless behavior, like its predecessors

quote: It will still make a significant number of shake-your-head stupid errors, in ways that are hard to fully predict
claim: GPT-5 will still make a significant number of unpredictable stupid errors

quote: Reasoning about physical, psychological and mathematical world will still be unreliable
claim: GPT-5's reasoning about the physical, psychological, and mathematical world will remain unreliable

quote: Fluent hallucinations will still be common, and easily induced
claim: GPT-5 will still commonly produce fluent hallucinations that are easily induced

quote: Its natural language output still won't be something that one can reliably hook up to downstream programs
claim: GPT-5's natural language output will not be reliably connectable to downstream programs like databases or virtual assistants

quote: GPT-5 by itself won't be a general purpose artificial general intelligence capable of taking on arbitrary tasks
claim: GPT-5 will not be a general-purpose AGI capable of arbitrary tasks

quote: "Alignment" between what humans want and what machines do will continue to be a critical, unsolved problem
claim: The alignment problem between human intentions and machine behavior will remain unsolved in GPT-5

quote: "Scaling" alone—building bigger and models until they absorb the entire internet — will prove useful, but only to a point
claim: Scaling LLMs alone will prove useful only up to a point and will not suffice for AGI

quote: Trustworthy, general artificial intelligence, aligned with human values, will come, when it does, from systems that are more structured, with more built-in knowledge, and will incorporate at least some degree of explicit tools for reasoning and planning
claim: Trustworthy AGI will require more structured systems with built-in knowledge and explicit reasoning and planning tools, not just LLMs

quote: Within a decade, maybe much less, the focus of AI will move from a pure focus on scaling large language models to a focus on integrating them with a wide range of other techniques
claim: Within a decade, AI focus will shift from pure LLM scaling to integrating LLMs with other techniques

quote: we will, I predict, need genuinely new architectures that incorporate explicit knowledge and world models at their very core
claim: Achieving trustworthy AI will require genuinely new architectures with explicit knowledge and world models at their core

trigger: AI agent launches by major companies in 2025
quote: AI "Agents" will be endlessly hyped throughout 2025 but far from reliable, except possibly in very narrow use cases
claim: AI agents will be far from reliable throughout 2025, except possibly in very narrow use cases

trigger: AI agent launches by major companies in 2025
quote: none, to my knowledge are reliable, except maybe in very narrow uses cases
claim: As of mid-2025, no major company's AI agent is reliable except possibly in very narrow use cases

trigger: AI agent launches by major companies in 2025
quote: AI coding agents also seem to be incurring a large amount of technical debt
claim: AI coding agents are incurring large amounts of technical debt by producing hard-to-debug copypasta code

trigger: AI agent launches by major companies in 2025
quote: AI errors tend to compound over time
claim: AI agent errors tend to compound over time rather than remain isolated

trigger: AI agent launches by major companies in 2025
quote: current agents are grossly vulnerable to cyberattacks
claim: Current AI agents are grossly vulnerable to cyberattacks due to their superficial understanding of tasks

trigger: AI agent launches by major companies in 2025
quote: What drives LLMs (and what drives the current batch of agents) is mimicry, and not what Ernest Davis and I called in 2019 deep understanding
claim: LLMs and current AI agents are driven by mimicry rather than deep understanding

trigger: AI agent launches by major companies in 2025
quote: agentic tasks often involve multiple steps. In fundamentally unreliable systems like LLMs, that means multiple chances for error. Soon or later, those errors catch up
claim: Multi-step agentic tasks in unreliable LLM-based systems inevitably accumulate errors that lead to failure

trigger: AI agent launches by major companies in 2025
quote: I seriously doubt that LLMs will ever yield the substrate we need
claim: LLMs will likely never provide the substrate needed for reliable AI agents

trigger: AI agent launches by major companies in 2025
quote: pure scaling is not getting us to AGI; returns are diminishing. GPT-5, they report, will not be the jump over GPT-4 that GPT-4 was over GPT-3
claim: Returns from pure scaling are diminishing; GPT-5 will not represent as large a jump over GPT-4 as GPT-4 was over GPT-3

trigger: AI agent launches by major companies in 2025
quote: Without neurosymbolic AI that is more deeply integrated in systems as a whole, with rich world models as a central component, generally following the approach I laid out five years ago, I just don't see how agents can work out
claim: AI agents cannot succeed without neurosymbolic AI with rich world models deeply integrated into the system

trigger: AI agent launches by major companies in 2025
quote: Reliable agents may not require "artificial general intelligence", but they surely require what I called in 2020 "robust", trustworthy AI. LLMs aren't getting us there
claim: Reliable AI agents require robust, trustworthy AI, which LLMs cannot provide

trigger: AI agent launches by major companies in 2025
quote: the failure of current agents to produce much of lasting value will be seen as a poignant reflection of a massive mistake, intellectually and economically speaking: the mistake of investing almost solely in LLMs
claim: The failure of current AI agents will eventually be seen as reflecting a massive mistake of investing almost solely in LLMs

trigger: AI agent launches by major companies in 2025
quote: alternative approaches like neurosymbolic AI continue to be wildly underfunded, receiving, if I had to guess maybe 1%, probably less, of total investments
claim: Neurosymbolic AI approaches receive roughly 1% or less of total AI investment, vastly underfunded relative to LLMs

quote: some significant fraction of that science will be wrong, because of the inevitable tendency of LLMs to hallucinate
claim: A significant fraction of AI-assisted science will be wrong due to the inevitable tendency of LLMs to hallucinate

quote: The AI industry magic sauce of "scaling" hasn't made the enshittification situation better; it has made it worse
claim: Scaling LLMs has worsened rather than improved the problem of AI-generated low-quality content polluting the internet

quote: Cesspools of automatically-generated fake websites, rather than ChatGPT search, may ultimately come to be the single biggest threat that Google ever faces
claim: AI-generated fake websites may be a bigger threat to Google than ChatGPT search competition

quote: still part of the pack, not a giant leap forward (e.g. Grok 4 beats it on ARC-AGI-2 results)
claim: GPT-5 is part of the pack of current AI models, not a giant leap forward, with Grok 4 beating it on ARC-AGI-2

quote: GPT-5 is not the huge leap forward people long expected
claim: GPT-5 is not the huge leap forward that was widely expected

quote: GPT-5 is obviously not AGI
claim: GPT-5 is obviously not AGI

quote: Pricing is good, but profits may continue to be elusive; still no clear technical moat
claim: Despite good pricing, OpenAI profits may continue to be elusive and the company lacks a clear technical moat

quote: "AGI 2027" seems more and more remote by the day
claim: The prospect of achieving AGI by 2027 is becoming increasingly unlikely

quote: none of the problems I said would not be solved appear to have been solved
claim: GPT-5 has not solved any of the fundamental problems Marcus predicted would remain unsolved (hallucinations, reasoning errors, alignment)

quote: GPT-5 just not that different from anything that came before
claim: GPT-5 is not qualitatively different from previous models

quote: GPT-5 is barely better than last month's flavor of the month (Grok 4); on some metrics (ARC-AGI-2) it's actually worse
claim: GPT-5 is barely better than Grok 4 and actually worse on some metrics like ARC-AGI-2

quote: They no longer have anything like a clear technical lead
claim: OpenAI no longer has a clear technical lead over competitors

quote: GPT-5 is unlikely to be ahead of the pack for more than a couple months
claim: GPT-5 is unlikely to remain the leading model for more than a couple of months

quote: OpenAI still isn't making profit. Instead they are being forced to cut prices
claim: OpenAI is still unprofitable and being forced to cut prices

quote: People are wising up that LLMs are not in fact AGI-adjacent
claim: Public perception is shifting toward recognizing that LLMs are not close to AGI

trigger: GPT-5 release and ASU study on distribution shift
quote: Chain of Thought reasoning is "a brittle mirage that vanishes when it is pushed beyond training distributions"
claim: Chain-of-thought reasoning in LLMs is a brittle capability that fails when pushed beyond training distributions

trigger: GPT-5 release and ASU study on distribution shift
quote: the failure to generalize adequately outside distribution tells us why all the dozens of shots on goal at building "GPT-5 level models" keep missing their target. It's not an accident. That failing is principled
claim: LLMs' failure to generalize outside their training distribution is a principled, systematic limitation explaining why successive models keep failing at the same problems

quote: GPT-5 may be a moderate quantitative improvement (and it may be cheaper) but it still fails in all the same qualitative ways as its predecessors, on chess, on reasoning, in vision; even sometimes on counting and basic math
claim: GPT-5 is a moderate quantitative improvement but fails in all the same qualitative ways as predecessors in chess, reasoning, vision, and basic math

quote: Pure scaling simply isn't the path to AGI. It turns out that attention, the key component in LLMs, and the focus of the justly famous Transformer paper, is not fact "all you need"
claim: Pure scaling of transformer-based LLMs is not the path to AGI

quote: All I am saying is give neurosymbolic AI with explicit world models a chance. Only once we have systems that can reason about enduring representations of the world, including but not to limited to abstract symbolic ones, will we have a genuine shot at AGI
claim: AGI requires neurosymbolic AI with explicit world models that can reason about enduring representations of the world

trigger: Xiao Ma viral tweet about LLM temporal reasoning
quote: LLMs never induce proper world models, which is why, for example, they still can't even play chess reliably
claim: LLMs never induce proper world models, which explains their inability to reliably play chess or perform other tasks requiring world knowledge

trigger: Xiao Ma viral tweet about LLM temporal reasoning
quote: LLMs are built, essentially, to autocomplete. Autocomplete as we know it works surprisingly well, for some purposes, but just isn't good enough for world model induction
claim: LLMs are fundamentally autocomplete systems, which is insufficient for world model induction

trigger: Xiao Ma viral tweet about LLM temporal reasoning
quote: If an AI system can't induce a robust model of something as simple and constant as the rules of chess, it's preposterous to think it is going to induce all it needs to know about time and finance and shopping and the dynamics of a changing every day world just from the statistical analysis of big data
claim: If LLMs cannot induce robust models of simple rule systems like chess, they cannot induce the complex world knowledge needed for everyday reasoning from statistical analysis alone

trigger: Xiao Ma viral tweet about LLM temporal reasoning
quote: They sound like us, but they don't think like us
claim: LLMs produce human-like language output but do not think or reason like humans

trigger: Xiao Ma viral tweet about LLM temporal reasoning
quote: no matter how much LLMs mimic the patterns of human language, they are not like us
claim: LLMs are fundamentally unlike humans regardless of how well they mimic human language patterns

trigger: Xiao Ma viral tweet about LLM temporal reasoning
quote: Always regard them like the weird function approximators they are; never trust them
claim: LLMs should be regarded as unreliable function approximators that should never be fully trusted

trigger: Xiao Ma viral tweet about LLM temporal reasoning
quote: There are many problems with using LLMs as the core of agents, ranging from security vulnerabilities to their increasingly evidence lack of reliability
claim: Using LLMs as the core of AI agents is problematic due to security vulnerabilities and unreliability

quote: GPT-5's disappointments could spark a new AI winter
claim: GPT-5's disappointing release could trigger a new AI winter

quote: There is a real sense in which GPT-5 is starting to look Altman's Waterloo
claim: GPT-5 may represent a defining defeat for Sam Altman's leadership and credibility

quote: Three years of hype add up, and Altman simply could not deliver what he (over)promised
claim: Altman overpromised on GPT-5 for three years and could not deliver

quote: the vast majority of academic AI researchers doubted that scaling would get us all the way to AGI
claim: The vast majority of academic AI researchers doubt that scaling alone will achieve AGI

quote: LLMs do markedly better on coding benchmarks than in real-word practice
claim: LLMs perform markedly better on coding benchmarks than in real-world coding practice

quote: Science is not a popularity contest; you can't bully your way to truth
claim: Scientific progress in AI cannot be achieved through hype and suppression of criticism

quote: AGI—hopefully safe, trustworthy AGI– will eventually come. Maybe in the next decade
claim: Safe, trustworthy AGI will eventually be achieved, possibly within the next decade

quote: LLMs won't lead the way
claim: LLMs will not be the technology that leads to AGI

trigger: Black Hat Las Vegas 2025 security conference
quote: two new technologies are radically increasing what is known as the attack surface (or the space for potential vulnerabilities): LLMs and coding agents
claim: LLMs and coding agents are radically increasing the cybersecurity attack surface

trigger: Black Hat Las Vegas 2025 security conference
quote: the number of ways to do this —engendering all sorts of negative consequences, including RCEs— was basically infinite
claim: The number of attack vectors for exploiting LLM-based coding agents is essentially infinite

trigger: Black Hat Las Vegas 2025 security conference
quote: As long as we have agents roaming the internet and otherwise incorporating data that they don't fully understand – and LLMs don't ever fully understand the data they are leveraging – there is enormous risk
claim: LLM-based agents that incorporate data they don't fully understand from the internet pose enormous security risks

trigger: Black Hat Las Vegas 2025 security conference
quote: high levels of agency, combined with the ease of manipulation of LLM-based software, is a recipe for chaos
claim: High agent autonomy combined with the ease of manipulating LLM-based software creates major security risks

trigger: Black Hat Las Vegas 2025 security conference
quote: if an attacker gets data into your generative AI system — by any means, and there are many, ranging from fake answers to online queries to fake software packages to fake data on fake pages and poisoned entries in widely-used RAG databases— then you can't trust the output
claim: If an attacker can inject data into a generative AI system by any means, the system's output cannot be trusted

trigger: Black Hat Las Vegas 2025 security conference
quote: Don't treat LLM coding agents as highly capable superintelligent systems
claim: LLM coding agents should not be treated as highly capable or superintelligent systems

trigger: Black Hat Las Vegas 2025 security conference
quote: Treat them as lazy, intoxicated robots
claim: LLM coding agents should be treated as lazy, unreliable systems prone to manipulation

trigger: Black Hat Las Vegas 2025 security conference
quote: Nathan and Nils were able to access the application's secrets, including the GitHub private key of the CodeRabbit application, as well as the unique install ID for repositories where CodeRabbit could run. When all was said and done, this left them with the ability to access over a million GitHub repositories
claim: Security researchers demonstrated they could exploit AI code review tools to gain access to over a million GitHub repositories, including write access

quote: LeCun was warm to LLMs when his company was in the lead, but once they lost the lead, he was hostile to LLM's thereafter
claim: Yann LeCun's stance on LLMs shifted from positive to hostile after Meta lost its competitive lead to ChatGPT

quote: his lead (and his reputation) has dwindled, after the disappointment of GPT-5. He is now on a mission to downscale the insane expectations that he built and couldn't meet
claim: After GPT-5's disappointing release, Altman is trying to walk back the extreme expectations he built up

quote: The technical foundation was, as I argued all the way back to the early days of GPT-2, never strong enough to support the hype
claim: The technical foundation of LLMs was never strong enough to support the hype surrounding them

quote: the economics don't make much sense either
claim: The economics of generative AI do not make much sense

quote: Once the markets really understand this, enthusiasm may indeed collapse fairly quickly
claim: When markets fully understand the gap between AI hype and reality, enthusiasm may collapse quickly

quote: Employers of late are often looking to cut employees. They often use vague talk of AI to license their layoffs, and they often don't know what the hell they are talking about, at least when it comes to AI
claim: Employers are frequently using vague claims about AI to justify layoffs without understanding AI's actual capabilities

quote: gutting junior employees, especially in fields such as software development, in favor of AI is (at least for now, until the tech is vastly more mature and reliable) a short-term play that could easily come back to bite employers
claim: Replacing junior employees with AI is a short-sighted strategy that will backfire because AI technology is not yet mature or reliable enough

quote: If we stop hiring junior employees, we risk destroying the pipeline to expertis
claim: Eliminating junior employee hiring in favor of AI risks destroying the pipeline that develops senior expertise

quote: In 2016 Geoff Hinton promised that we no longer need to training radiologists. Almost a decade later, not one (to my knowledge) has been replaced
claim: Despite Hinton's 2016 prediction, almost a decade later no radiologists have been replaced by AI

quote: all too often I believe that they are underestimating what humans can do and overestimating the technological readiness of current AI, which can often do large portions of a job but not the whole thing
claim: Companies replacing workers with AI are underestimating human capabilities and overestimating AI's technological readiness, which can handle portions of jobs but not complete ones

trigger: Google Nano Banana image editor release
quote: scaling can only take you so far
claim: Scaling AI models can only improve performance up to a point, beyond which fundamental limitations remain

trigger: Google Nano Banana image editor release
quote: parts and wholes would pose problems in systems that rely on statistics without deep world knowledge
claim: AI systems relying on statistics without deep world knowledge will continue to struggle with compositionality (parts and wholes)

trigger: Google Nano Banana image editor release
quote: Over the last few years, graphics have gotten better and better. World models and comprehension less so
claim: AI image generation graphics have improved substantially, but world models and comprehension have not improved comparably

trigger: Google Nano Banana image editor release
quote: Scaling works, but only to a degree, and only on some aspects of intelligence and not others
claim: Scaling improves some aspects of AI (like graphics quality) but not others (like world understanding and compositionality)

trigger: Google Nano Banana image editor release
quote: it's all still just an extended form of mimicry, not something deeper, as the persistent failures with parts and wholes keep showing us
claim: AI image generation remains an extended form of mimicry rather than deep understanding, as shown by persistent compositionality failures

quote: These systems can draw things that kinda look like the world, but they obviously don't know what's what or how things work outside of their training data
claim: Generative AI image systems can produce visually plausible images but lack understanding of how objects actually work

quote: Better graphics doesn't necessarily entail a better understanding of the world
claim: Improvements in AI-generated graphics quality do not necessarily reflect improved understanding of the world

quote: they live in the land of correlations between images and text, not in the land of ideas
claim: Generative AI image systems operate on statistical correlations between images and text, not on conceptual understanding

trigger: Marcus NYT essay on AI rethink
quote: Scaling hasn't gotten us to AGI, or 'superintelligence", let alone AI we could trust
claim: Scaling has failed to produce AGI, superintelligence, or trustworthy AI

trigger: Marcus NYT essay on AI rethink
quote: The field is overdue for a rethink
claim: The AI field is overdue for a fundamental rethink of its approach beyond scaling

quote: The notion on which this Substack was founded in May 2022 — that LLM scaling would not bring us to AGI — has gone mainstream
claim: The view that LLM scaling alone cannot achieve AGI has become mainstream consensus

quote: the problem of wildly derivative outputs has not gone away
claim: The problem of AI generating wildly derivative outputs that potentially violate copyrights has not been resolved

quote: the Jurassic Park moment of mass-produced AI-generated misinformation that I projected in December 2022 has arrived
claim: The era of mass-produced AI-generated misinformation has arrived

quote: If there is any true exponential nowadays, I fear it is this: the rapid doubling of AI-generated misinformation
claim: AI-generated misinformation is growing exponentially

quote: my December 2022 predictions of deaths by chatbot have now also been confirmed, more than once
claim: Chatbot-related deaths, predicted by Marcus in December 2022, have now occurred multiple times

trigger: OpenAI revenue projections and financial reporting
quote: they are expecting to burn $150 billion between 2025 and 2030
claim: OpenAI expects to spend $150 billion on computing costs alone between 2025 and 2030

trigger: OpenAI revenue projections and financial reporting
quote: OpenAI can't make its ambitious revenue numbers — 13x-ing current revenue in five years — unless revenue from paying big business customers keeps going up and up and up and up and up
claim: OpenAI's projected 13x revenue growth in five years requires implausibly sustained growth in enterprise customers

trigger: Census Bureau data on AI adoption
quote: If things go up and up until they don't, OpenAI is toast
claim: If enterprise AI adoption growth stalls, OpenAI's business model will fail

quote: OpenAI doesn't have $300 billion dollars
claim: OpenAI does not have $300 billion and is far from profitable, making its massive AI infrastructure deals questionable

quote: If Oracle actually collects its $300 billion, I will truly be astounded
claim: Oracle is very unlikely to actually collect $300 billion from its announced AI contracts

quote: We are well past peak bubble, in fact, and into peak musical chairs. It's not going to be pretty when the music stops
claim: The AI industry has passed peak bubble and a painful correction is coming

quote: Oracle's new market cap, near a trillion dollars, up nearly 50% this week, driven largely by this one apparently non-binding deal with a party that doesn't have the money to pay for the services, seems more bonkers than most
claim: Oracle's near-trillion-dollar market cap, driven by a non-binding deal with a party that cannot pay, is irrational

quote: the total value of the tech market as whole, which is supposed to reflect the future of value of the companies within it, far exceeds what is likely ever to be delivered
claim: The total tech market valuation far exceeds the value that AI companies are likely to ever deliver

trigger: UN General Assembly letter on AI red lines
quote: We urge governments to reach an international agreement on red lines for AI — ensuring they are operational, with robust enforcement mechanisms — by the end of 2026
claim: Governments should reach an international agreement on AI red lines with robust enforcement mechanisms by end of 2026

trigger: UN General Assembly letter on AI red lines
quote: we have let too much slide, and done too little to face the risks
claim: Governments and society have done too little to address the risks posed by AI
